title: Why Shakespeare Invented 1,700 Words We Still Use

imageCredit.creator: Attributed to John Taylor; National Portrait Gallery

summary: Shakespeare did not single-handedly invent modern English, but his plays and poems helped preserve, popularize, and display an extraordinary range of words.

lesson.learn: The claim that Shakespeare invented 1,700 words needs nuance. Scholars often mean that his works contain the first recorded uses of many words in surviving English sources, not that he personally coined every one from nothing. Early modern English was unusually flexible: writers converted nouns into verbs, added prefixes and suffixes, borrowed from Latin and French, and experimented with compound forms. Shakespeare was exceptionally visible because his plays were printed, performed, quoted, and later indexed by dictionaries. His genius was not just making words, but placing them in dramatic situations where audiences could instantly feel their force.
lesson.simpler: Shakespeare's works preserve many early uses of English words, and his popularity helped those words and phrases travel widely.
lesson.keyTakeaway: His linguistic legacy comes from turning the experimental energy of early modern English into memorable dramatic speech that later readers, actors, and lexicographers kept alive.
lesson.deeperDive: Because dictionaries rely on written evidence, famous authors can appear to 'invent' words that may already have circulated in speech. That does not diminish Shakespeare's achievement. Words such as 'lonely,' 'majestic,' and 'bedazzled' are powerful in his work because they arrive inside conflicts of love, ambition, jealousy, grief, and performance. The plays also made language itself theatrical: puns, insults, metaphors, legalisms, songs, and rhetorical games become tools by which characters test power and identity.
lesson.funFact: The First Folio preserved many plays that might otherwise have been lost.
